terms.definition: Any computational system capable of generating language-level or analytical output that imitates human cognition; here restricted to large language models such as GPT-4.
The simultaneous presence of mutually exclusive claims within the same evidentiary field.
The degree to which component propositions of a system mutually support each other to form a non-self-contradictory whole.
A hypothesised downward trend in collectively endorsed ethical norms, operationalised via metrics such as violent-crime rate, family-stability indices, and institutional-trust surveys.
An interdisciplinary framework synthesising theological premises with physical-systems analysis to study reality.
An intentionally designed workflow that structures the researcher’s information-processing steps so as to minimise bias and maximise evidential integrity.
An artefact, dataset, or original document created contemporaneously with the event or phenomenon under study, unmediated by secondary interpretation.
The alteration of a phenomenon caused by the act of observing or measuring it; here, the researcher’s prompts may change the AI’s subsequent outputs, necessitating explicit documentation.
terms.first_use_section: What AI Does — and Does Not Do
